segments.s054-g113: زمین را is the definite object (زمین + object marker را) 'the ground'; ببوسید is a past tense verb 'he kissed'. The conjunction و links that clause to کرد آفرین: here کرد acts as a light verb 'made/uttered' and آفرین is an acclamation '“bravo”' — together کرد آفرین = 'uttered “afrin”/praised'.
segments.s054-g114: بران is an archaic contraction of بر آن meaning 'on/for that' or 'upon him'; it introduces the things being praised. The following nouns are simply enumerated objects (تاج, تخت, کلاه, نگین) joined by و 'and'.
segments.s054-g115: فریدونش is Fereydun with an enclitic -ش (to/for him), so 'Fereydun said to him'. فرمود is the past-tense polite verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here introduces the subordinate clause meaning 'that/so that / when' (classical usage) and برنشست is a past form 'sat upon / took his seat (on it)'.
segments.s054-g116: ببوسید is 'he kissed' (past). The conjunction و links the next verb بسترد which here means 'covered/veiled' (i.e., hid). رویش is 'its/his face' (روی + enclitic ش), and به دست is an instrumental phrase 'with [his] hand' (به used here in the sense 'by/with'). Together: 'he kissed it and covered his face with his hand.'
segments.s054-g117: پس آنگه are consecutive adverbials 'then'/'after that' for emphasis. سوی آسمان is the directional phrase 'toward heaven/sky'. کرد + روی is a verb + noun construction meaning 'made (his) face' i.e. 'turned his face' (کرد used as light verb and روی 'face' as the thing moved).
segments.s054-g118: که‌ای here is an archaic/exclamatory vocative (equivalent to 'که ای' → 'O'); it introduces the direct address. The following three words are epithets in apposition: دادگر 'just', داور 'judge', راست‌گوی 'truthful/speaker of truth', forming a vocative list directed upward.
segments.s054-g119: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. گفتی is the past tense 'you said'. که introduces the quoted content 'that…'. من is 'I', and دادگر داورم is a predicate: دادگر 'just' and داورم 'I am judge' (داور + -م first-person suffix) — together 'I am a just judge.'
segments.s054-g120: به سختی is an adverbial 'with severity / severely' (به + سختی). ستم دیده is a compound: ستم 'oppression/injustice' + دیده (past participle 'seen' → 'one who has been subjected to'), so ستم‌دیده means 'one who has suffered injustice'. را is the object marker attaching to that phrase, and یاورم is 'I am a helper/ally' (یاور + -م first-person). The clause means 'I am the helper of the one severely oppressed.'
segments.s054-g9: This is a noun phrase describing the subject: 'پر از خشم و پر کینه' is an adjectival construction meaning 'full of anger and full of grudge/hatred' (پر + از + noun). Word order in Persian places the modifiers before the head 'سالار نو' (the new commander). 'نو' (new) follows the head as a typical postpositive adjective in classical style.
segments.s054-g10: نشست is past tense 'sat' (3rd‑person singular). The prepositional phrase از بر + چرمه marks the locus ('upon/on the hide/saddle'); بر here functions 'on/upon' and چرمه names the leather hide or saddle‑cover. تیزرو is a postmodifier 'swift' describing the steed/runner; the whole segment says he sat on (the) swift one's hide.
segments.s054-g11: بیفگند is a past tense verb ('he threw/cast off'); here it has the sense of discarding or flinging aside (often armour or a cloak). It is coordinated with the following verb by و 'and'. بتاخت is another past tense verb (from تختن/تاختن) meaning 'he rushed/charged' or 'he galloped off' — both verbs describe successive actions by the same subject.
segments.s054-g12: به گرد سپه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'around the host/army' (به + گرد = 'around/round'). چرمه اندر is a locative phrase 'among/within the hides/leathers' (اندر = in/within), and نشاخت is an archaic/poetic form here giving the sense of making a commotion or settling there (the lemma list marks it as an archaic form). The clause as a whole describes his action encircling or stirring among the army near the hide.
segments.s054-g13: رسید is simple past 'he arrived/reached.' آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon.' تنگ در is a noun phrase 'narrow gate' (تنگ = narrow and در = gate here), and شاه روم is a genitival phrase 'the king of Rome' (شاه modifying روم). Word order is chronological adverb → verb → place phrase.
segments.s054-g14: خروشید is a past tense intransitive verb 'he roared/cried out.' The following phrase is direct exclamation: کای is the vocative 'O!', مرد is 'man' or 'hero' addressed, and بیداد شوم is a fixed exclamatory formula in which بیداد ('woe/injustice') combines with شوم (optative/undesirable 'may I become' or 'may I be') — effectively an outcry meaning 'O man, woe befall me!' or 'O man, may I be undone!'
segments.s054-g15: بکشتی here is past second person 'you killed' (not the preposition 'by ship'); برادر is direct object 'brother.' ز بهر is a two‑word prepositional expression 'for the sake of' (ز = because of, بهر = for), and کلاه is the motive/object 'a cap.' The segment accuses someone of killing his brother for a mere hat.
segments.s054-g16: کله یافتی is 'you obtained/found a head' (کله = head; یافتی = you found/obtained, past 2nd person). چند is the interrogative 'how many/how much' and پویی براه is a phrase meaning 'paces/steps on the road' or 'along the way' (براه = en route/toward the road); the speaker rhetorically asks how many steps along the road the head was found. The line combines accusation with a scornful question about the result.
segments.s054-g145: بپدرود کردنش is a compound consisting of the lexical element بپدرود 'to bid farewell' plus the light verb کردن; ش is the clitic object 'him/it'. Together they mean 'to bid him farewell'. رفتند پیش: رفتند is past plural 'they went'; پیش here is a locative/adverb meaning 'before/in the presence of' and combines with رفتند to mean 'they went up to' or 'they went before (him)'.
segments.s054-g146: چنان چون بود is a fixed, archaic/poetic correlative meaning 'as it was' (چنان… چون = 'such as' / 'as'). بود is the past copula 'was'. رسم آیین و کیش is a coordinated noun phrase listing near-synonyms (رسم 'rite/ceremony', آیین 'ritual', کیش 'custom/practice'), functioning as the thing 'as was' expressed by بود.
segments.s054-g147: در دخمه is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the vault/crypt'. بستند is the past plural 'they closed/fastened/bound'. بر شهریار follows as the direct target ('upon/against the king'), so the whole clause means 'they shut/closed (him) in the vault' with Persian flexible order placing the locative first.
segments.s054-g148: شد is the past/resultative 'became'. آن ارجمند is the noun phrase 'that noble/esteemed one'. از جهان here literally 'from/of the world' marks the sphere (i.e. in relation to the world). زار و خوار are coordinated adjectives 'wretched (زار) and despised (خوار)', completing the predicate 'became wretched and despised'.
segments.s054-g149: جهانا is the vocative 'O world'. سراسر 'throughout/entirely' modifies what follows. فسوسی is here used in the sense of remorse/regret (a poetic noun/nominalized form), and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (common in classical Persian) — together expressing a wish: 'O world, may there be remorse everywhere.'
segments.s054-g150: بتو is the prepositional phrase 'for you/to you'. نیست is the negative existential 'is not / there is not'. مرد خردمند شاد is a noun phrase 'a man — wise, happy' with adjectives following the noun in the usual Persian order, so the clause means 'for you there is no happy, wise man.'
segments.s054-g121: همم is an emphatic/old-fashioned object form meaning “to me (also)” (an enclitic 'هم' + first‑person marker). داد here is the abstract noun ‘justice’ (داد, not the verb). دادی is the 2nd‑person past singular verb ‘you gave’. The clause order is object (همم داد) + verb (دادی), a common poetic/narrative pattern; و links the two parallel objects, with هم before داوری marking ‘also’.
segments.s054-g122: Again همم = ‘to me (also)’. تاج is the noun ‘crown’ and دادی is the same 2nd‑person past verb ‘you gave’. The following هم (here ‘also/too’) links تاج and انگشتری (ring) as coordinated gifts; انگشتری is the direct object. Word order (object + verb) and repeated هم/همم produce the parallel, emphatic listing typical of poetic diction.
segments.s054-g123: بفرمود is the honorific/politer past narrative verb ‘he said/ordered’. پس is a connective ‘then/thereupon’. تا functions here as a conjunction introducing the content/result clause (‘that/so that’). منوچهر شاه is the full name + title (subject of the following clause).
segments.s054-g124: نشست is the past of نشستن ‘sat/sit’; in narrative after an order it records that the action took place. از ... بر is an archaic/prepositional pairing (از بر) meaning roughly ‘upon/on’ the throne; together they mark location. تخت زر is a genitive/attributive phrase ‘throne of gold’ (i.e. golden throne). با کلاه is a prepositional adjunct ‘with (a) hat/crown’ indicating he is wearing it; in older Persian کلاه can mean a king’s headgear.
segments.s054-g125: سپهدار is a title meaning ‘commander’ and functions as a nominal head before the name. شیروی here is an epithet or name (literally ‘lion‑like’); it follows the title as part of the named person. با introduces accompaniment or manner; خواسته is a noun ‘request/petition’ (so با خواسته = ‘with a petition’ or ‘bringing a request’).
segments.s054-g126: به درگاه شاه is the prepositional phrase ‘to the king’s court/presence’ (به + درگاه). آمد is the past tense verb ‘came’. آراسته is a predicative adjective/participle ‘adorned’ placed after the verb as a resultative/depictive modifier (he came, adorned). The word order (prepositional phrase + آمد + adjective) is typical in narrative.
segments.s054-g127: As before, بفرمود is the honorific past ‘he commanded/said’, پس is ‘then’, تا introduces the content/result clause ‘that’, and منوچهر شاه is the subject of the subordinate clause (the king who will perform the action).
segments.s054-g128: ببخشید is the past tense ‘forgave/pardoned’ (here used of the king). یکسر is an adverb meaning ‘entirely/at once’ modifying the verb. همه is the object ‘all/the whole (group)’. با سپاه is an adverbial phrase ‘with the army’ (either indicating accompaniment or that the army was included); together the phrase says the king pardoned everyone completely, along with the army.
segments.s054-g25: همی is a literary continuous adverb meaning ‘kept on’ and precedes the verb; تاخت is the simple past ‘galloped/charged’. In classical Persian verse the verb often comes before its subject, so اسپ follows تاخت as the subject. اندرین گفت‌گوی is a locative prepositional phrase (‘in/into this mêlée/encounter’) that specifies where the horse charged.
segments.s054-g26: یکایک is an adverbial ‘one by one’ modifying the following clause. به تنگی رسید is a past-clause meaning ‘came to/was brought into straitened circumstances’ or ‘came up in the narrow press’; رسید is past simple (3rd‑singular here because each individual is meant). اندر اوی is a locative/target phrase ‘in/at him’ (اوی = ‘him/it’), showing who is the focus of that coming up.
segments.s054-g27: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one' and governs the singular past verb زد. تیغ زد is the common verbal phrase 'struck with a sword'; زود is an adverb ‘quickly/at once’ modifying زد. بر گردنش is the prepositional object phrase ‘upon/at his neck’ (گردن + enclitic ش = 'his neck').
segments.s054-g28: بدو is the dative/clarifying pronoun 'to him/unto it' marking the person affected. نیمه شد is a resultative construction 'became half / was halved' (شد = 'became' in past). خسروانی تنش is a noun phrase 'his royal/kingly body' (خسروانی modifying تن, with ش enclitic 'his'), the patient of the result.
segments.s054-g29: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces the content/result clause here ('that/so that'), so تا سرش برداشتند = 'that they should take his head'. سرش is 'his head' (سر + ش enclitic), and برداشتند is past 3rd plural 'they took/removed'—it agrees with the plural executors of the order.
segments.s054-g30: The phrase uses two instances of به to indicate instrument/direction: به نیزه 'upon/onto spears' (instrumental/locative) and به ابر 'toward/up to the cloud/sky' (direction). اندر here reinforces the locative/directional sense ('into/within'), and افراشتند is past 3rd plural 'they raised/erected'—so the clause means they raised it upon spears up into the clouds.
segments.s054-g31: بماندند is past 3rd plural 'they remained/stood' and appears before its subject (a common poetic inversion): لشکر شگفت is the subject phrase 'the amazed/marveling host' (شگفت modifying لشکر). اندر اوی is the locative/target 'at him/around him', so the whole clause says the host stood amazed at him.
segments.s054-g32: This is a compact explanatory noun phrase introduced by ازان 'from/for that' giving the cause: 'ازان زور' = 'because of that strength' and 'و آن بازوی جنگجوی' = 'and that warrior arm' (آن pointing back, بازوی modified by جنگجوی). Persian poetry often omits linking particles; here the nouns are simply coordinated to give the reason for the host's amazement.
segments.s054-g41: بگوید is a third-person present/subjunctive (used narratively as a formulaic 'he says' or 'he declared') introducing reported speech; که is the complementizer 'that' linking the reporting clause to the reported clause; گفتند is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they said'; ما کهتریم is the reported utterance 'we are inferior' where کهتریم is an adjective with the 1st-person-plural ending -یم (copula omitted, as is normal in Persian). Word order is reporting-connector → reported clause (subject + predicate).
segments.s054-g42: This clause is the continuation of the reported speech: زمین 'the land' is the object, جز 'except' restricts the following prepositional phrase, به فرمان او 'to/by his command' is a prepositional phrase marking the only acceptable condition, and نسپریم is 1st-person-plural present (here negative) of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' with the negative prefix ن-, giving 'we do not/will not entrust'. The literal order is object → restriction → prepositional condition → verb.
segments.s054-g43: گروهی is an indefinite distributive 'some' or 'a group (of people)'; خداوند here means 'lord/master/sovereign' and functions as the predicate nominal; بر is the preposition 'over' indicating jurisdiction and چارپای 'quadrupeds' (livestock) is the object of that jurisdiction. The copula ('were') is omitted as common in Persian, so the phrase reads 'some were lords over quadrupeds.'
segments.s054-g44: This is a parallel clause: again گروهی 'some' + خداوند 'were masters' with the omitted copula; کشت is used as a noun here ('cultivation' / 'crops, fields') and pairs with سرای to list domains of mastery ('of crops and of houses'). The conjunction و connects the two items. (The clause remains elliptical with the verb 'were' understood.)
segments.s054-g45: سپاهی 'soldiers/we as soldiers' is the subject (or vocative group-identifying noun); بدین is an archaic compound (به + این) meaning 'to this' or 'into this' and governs رزمگاه 'battlefield' (بدین رزمگاه = 'to this battlefield'); آمدیم is the 1st-person-plural past/simple perfect 'we came.' The order is subject → to-this-place → verb.
segments.s054-g46: The clause begins with a negation (نه) meaning 'not' (present in the segment text); بر آرزو is a prepositional phrase 'for/by desire' and کینه‌خواه is a compound adjective 'desiring revenge/seeking a grudge' (کینه 'grudge/hatred' + خواه 'wanting'); آمدیم repeats the 1st-person-plural past 'we came.' Together the sense is 'we did not come here seeking revenge.' Word order places the negation and purpose phrase before the predicate.
segments.s054-g47: کنون 'now' sets the time; سر به سر is an idiom meaning 'entirely' or 'head to toe' (intensifier); شاه را uses را as the definite object marker with شاه 'the king' as the object of devotion; بنده‌ایم is 1st-person-plural present 'we are servants/devotees.' The copula is contained in the -ایم ending and the object marker را emphasizes 'the king' as the one to whom they are servants.
segments.s054-g48: دل و جان pairs two nouns 'heart and life/soul' as the internal objects; به مهر وی is a prepositional phrase 'with/for his love' (به + مهر + وی = 'filled with love of him'); آگنده‌ایم is the 1st-person-plural present passive/adjectival 'we are filled' (آگنده 'filled' + -ایم 'we are'), so the phrase means 'our heart and soul are filled with love for him.'
segments.s054-g73: ازین پس (از + این + پس) is an archaic fused phrase meaning “from now on / henceforth.” به introduces the manner phrase خیره (literally “staring/astonished”) and here gives an adverbial sense “in/with astonishment.” مریزید is the negative imperative/form of ریختن (with the prefix ب- often preserved in older poetic forms) meaning “do not shed/let fall,” and خون is the direct object “blood.” Word order is topical/poetic: temporal phrase first, then manner, then the verb and its object.
segments.s054-g74: که is the subordinating conjunction “for/because,” introducing the reason clause. بخت here means fate/fortune (the larger destiny), جفاپیشگان is a plural noun meaning “the oppressors/cruel ones,” شد is the past tense of شدن “became,” and نگون is an adjective meaning “ill‑fated/dooméd.” Together: “for the fate of the oppressors has become ill‑fated/doomed.”
segments.s054-g75: همه is the quantifier “all/every,” modifying the coordinated noun phrase آلت لشکر (“implements/equipment of the army”) and ساز جنگ (“instruments/devices of war”). آلت لشکر is a genitive-like construction (instrument + army) meaning army equipment; ساز جنگ is a nominal compound “war‑instruments/devices.”
segments.s054-g76: ببردند is the past tense 3rd‑person plural of بردن “they took/carried (away).” نزدیک functions adverbially/locatively “near / toward,” and پور پشنگ is a personal name (پور used here as the name‑forming element). The object of بردند (the instruments mentioned) is understood/represented earlier; placement of the adverbial before the name is a poetic ordering.
segments.s054-g77: سپهبد is the title “spahbed” (general) followed by the name منوچهر as subject. بنواختشان is a past form with the older prefix ب‑ + نواخت (to play/strike) plus the object clitic ‑شان “them,” meaning “he played/sounded them” or “had them played.” The verb form is transitive and carries the object suffix.
segments.s054-g78: براندازه (بر اندازه) is an adverbial phrase “in proportion / according to measure.” بر is the preposition “on/upon,” پایگه is “base/platform,” and ساختشان is ساخت + object clitic ‑شان “he made/placed them.” Together it expresses that he ordered/arranged them properly on the platform (built/placed them on the base).
segments.s054-g79: سوی دژ is a prepositional phrase “toward the fortress” (سوی = toward(s), دژ = fortress). فرستاد is the past tense “he sent.” شیروی followed by the object marker را marks the name Shiruy as the direct object of فرستاد; the following segment is an appositive/epithet modifying that object.
segments.s054-g80: جهاندیده مرد جهانجوی را is an appositive string of epithets modifying Shiruy. جهاندیده (“world‑seen/experienced”) and جهانجوی (“world‑seeking/ambitious”) qualify مرد (“man”), and the final را again marks the entire phrase as the (emphatic) object. This is a poetic stacked epithet structure: ‘Shiruy, the experienced, world‑seeking man.’
segments.s054-g17: کنون is a temporal adverb (‘now’) placed at the start for emphasis. تاجت is the possessed noun (‘your crown’) with the enclitic -ت; آوردم is a first‑person past tense verb ‘I brought’ (subject omitted). ای شاه is a vocative inserted after the object, and و تخت simply adds ‘and the throne’ as a second object.
segments.s054-g18: به بار آمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning ‘to come into bearing / to bear fruit’; here به functions as the directional/idiomatic particle and آمد is the past tense ‘came’ used with بار (‘crop/fruit’). آن خسروانی درخت is a determiner + adjective + noun sequence ‘that royal tree’ following the verb phrase.
segments.s054-g19: زتاج بزرگی is a genitival/ adjectival cluster meaning roughly ‘the greatness of the crown’ (زتاج = 'of the crown' / crowned). گریزان مشو is a prohibition: گریزان is a participial adjective ‘fleeing/avoiding’ and مشو is the negative imperative/prohibitive (‘do not become / do not be’), so together ‘do not be avoiding/shrink from…’.
segments.s054-g20: فریدونت is a possessive form (‘your Fereydun’ — a name/epithet used here); گاهی is an adverb ‘sometimes’. بیاراست is a past/perfective form of ‘to adorn/arrange’ (third singular here), and نو postpositively modifies it as ‘anew/new’. Word order places the agent/possessive noun before the temporal adverb and verb.
segments.s054-g21: درختی که ... is a relative clause: درختی (a tree) + که (that/which) introduces the clause. پروردی is the past, 2nd-person singular form 'you nurtured/raised' (archaic/poetic ending -ی on the past). آمد به بار is an idiom: آمد (came) + به بار (to bearing/a load) = 'has come to bear fruit' (i.e. 'has borne fruit').
segments.s054-g22: بیابی is the 2nd-person subjunctive/imperative-like form (prefix ب- + یاب) used here as 'you will/may find' or an address 'find...'. هم‌اکنون is the adverb 'right now'. برش is a nominal form ('part/section' or 'its part') and is followed by در کنار 'at/by the side'; together برش در کنار means 'its part/section at the side' (i.e. 'you will find it now beside [it/him]').
segments.s054-g23: اگر marks a conditional 'if'. بار خارست is a compressed predication where خارست contains the archaic copular suffix -ست ('is'), so 'the load/yield is thorny'. خود is emphatic 'yourself/indeed'. کشته‌ای is a noun 'kashte/planter, cultivator' with the 2nd-person ending -ی giving 'you are a tiller' (copular sense without an explicit verb).
segments.s054-g24: وگر = وَ اگر 'and if / but if' continuing the conditional. پرنیانست again uses the adjectival root (پرنیان 'silken') plus the old copular -ست meaning 'is silken/of silk'. خود رشته‌ای parallels the previous line: خود 'yourself' + رشته (strand/thread) + the 2nd-person ending -ای gives 'you are a strand', i.e. 'you yourself are the strand/line'.
segments.s054-g97: چه با is an exclamatory prepositional phrase: چه (exclamatory 'how/what') + با ('with'), introducing the means or accompaniment. گونه گونه is a reduplicated adjective phrase meaning 'of many kinds/various', placed after با. درفشان is an adjectival/epithet 'shining, glittering' modifying the noun درفش (banner/standard) that follows; Persian poetic word order allows these modifiers in this sequence.
segments.s054-g98: جهانی شده is a compound where جهانی ('universal/whole') combines with شده (past participle/auxiliary 'has become/was made') to mean 'was made whole/all over' or 'the whole place became'. The following series سرخ و زرد و بنفش simply lists colors joined by و ('and') and functions as the complement/result of 'جهانی شده' — 'became red and yellow and purple.'
segments.s054-g99: ز is the poetic form of 'from' introducing the origin ('from the sea of Gilan'). دریای گیلان is a genitive construction ('the sea of Gilan'). چو ('like/as') introduces the simile, and ابر سیاه ('black cloud') is the image compared to the approaching host.
segments.s054-g100: دمادم ('continually/ever and anon') and بساری ('in abundance/in great numbers') are adverbial modifiers placed before the verb رسید ('arrived'); Persian poetry often places such adverbs ahead of the verb. رسید is the past-tense verb 'arrived', and آن سپاه ('that army/host') appears after the verb as the subject/topic, a common word order in Persian narrative poetry.
segments.s054-g101: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when (it) came' with چو = 'when/as' and آمد = past 'came'. بنزدیک شاه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'near the king' (بنزدیک = 'near, close at hand' + شاه = 'the king'). آن سپاه ('that host') is the subject of آمد, placed after the adverbial phrase as is common in poetic narrative.
segments.s054-g102: فریدون is the proper name (subject). پذیره here functions as a participial/qualifying word meaning 'for receiving/welcoming' (i.e., 'to receive them' or 'ready to receive'), so it conveys purpose or role. بیامد is the past tense 'came' (poetic b‑form of آمد) and براه ('on/to the road') is an adverbial phrase meaning 'onto the way' or 'forth onto the road'.
segments.s054-g103: همه 'all/every' quantifies گیل مردان ('Gilan men' — people of Gilan). چو شیر یله is a simile: چو = 'like/as', شیر = 'lion', یله (reduplicated adjective) means 'loose/unrestrained, wild' — so 'like wild/untamed lions'. Word order places the simile directly after the noun phrase for vivid comparison.
segments.s054-g104: ابا here occurs before the following noun phrase and functions like 'with' or 'wearing' (a poetic variant of با); however the provided lemma senses do not include this reading, so its exact dictionary sense is not listed. طوق زرین is a noun + adjective ('a golden collar' — طوق 'collar/necklace' + زرین 'golden'), and مشکین کله is a compound 'black-/dark-headed' (مشکین 'dark/black' modifying کله 'head'), describing their appearance or gear.
segments.s054-g1: تهی شد ز کینه سر کینه دار is an inverted predication: تهی شد (past of شدن) is the verb phrase 'became empty', followed by ز کینه 'of/from hate' giving the thing emptied; سرِ کینه‌دار is the logical subject placed after the predicate (a poetic inversion) and reads 'the grudge-bearing head/chief'. کینه‌دار is a compound where کینه names the hateful feeling and دار functions adjectivally as 'possessing/holding' (archaic/poetic formation).
segments.s054-g2: گریزان همی رفت سوی حصار: همی + past verb (رفت) is the epic/classical progressive or continuous past (he kept/was going); گریزَان is a present-participial adjective 'fleeing' that describes the subject (the same 'سر کینه‌دار' from the previous clause), سوی is the preposition 'toward', and حصار is the destination 'fortress'. Word order places adverbial/participial phrase before verb as is common in this register.
segments.s054-g3: پس اندر سپاه منوچهر شاه is a locative/temporal frame: پس 'then', اندر 'in/within', followed by سپاهِ منوچهرِ شاه (the genitive construction placing the owner's name and title after the possessed noun): 'the army of Manuchehr the king'. The order (noun + possessor + title) is typical in older Persian.
segments.s054-g4: دمان و دنان برگرفتند راه: the two coordinated terms before the verb act as the subject (they). برگرفتند is past plural 'they took up/assumed' (here 'set out on' or 'took up' the road), and راه is the direct object 'the road/path'. Word order is Subject — Verb — Object, with برگرفتند carrying past tense and plural agreement.
segments.s054-g5: چو شد سلم تا پیش دریا کنار: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause, شد (past of شدن) here 'came to / reached' in narrative usage, and سلم is the subject (a proper name). تا پیش دریا کنار means 'as far as the front/edge of the sea'—تا used as 'as far as', پیش 'before/in front of', دریا 'sea', کنار 'edge/bank'. The clause thus states 'when Salm reached the seashore'.
segments.s054-g6: ندید آنچه کشتی برآن رهگذار: ندید is simple past negative 'did not see'; آنچه is the relative/indefinite 'that which/whatever' functioning as the object of ندید; the remainder (کشتی برآن رهگذار) is a relative clause describing 'that which' — here reading as 'the ship that was passing there' with کشتی as subject, برآن 'at/on that (place/spot)', and رهگذار as 'passer-by/passing' used attributively. The whole means 'he saw not what ship was passing there.'
segments.s054-g7: چنان شد ز بس کشته و خسته دشت: چنان شد 'it became so'; ز بس introduces cause/degree 'from so much / because of the abundance of'; کشته و خسته are coordinated nouns/adjectives 'the slain and the exhausted', and دشت (placed at the end) is the affected noun 'the plain' (i.e. 'the plain was so [because of the many dead and exhausted]'). The line uses compact, slightly inverted epic word order where the verb precedes the affected noun.
segments.s054-g8: که پوینده را راه دشوار گشت: که introduces the result clause here ('so that'); پوینده را marks the affected person with the accusative/topic marker را ('the traveler'), راه is the subject of the following predicate, دشوار گشت is a two-word predicate meaning 'became difficult' (گشت = became). Literal order: 'that for the traveler the road became difficult.'
segments.s054-g33: همه (adverb) + لشکر (noun) names the entire force: 'all (of) the army'. سلم here is a proper name (Salm) attached after لشکر as a genitive/identifying noun; the lemma senses given do not include the proper-name use, so treat it as a name modifying لشکر. هم‍چون introduces a simile 'like / as'. رمه is the image 'flock/herd' used figuratively.
segments.s054-g34: که is the relative 'that/which' introducing the clause describing the flock. بپراگند is formed with the prefix ب- + present stem and here functions as a 3rd-person verb in a subjunctive/relative sense ('scatters' / 'is scattered'); in classical poetry this prefix marks the present/subjunctive/jussive aspect. روزگار here means 'fate' rather than neutral 'time', and دمه is 'breath' (i.e. 'the breath of fate').
segments.s054-g35: برفتند is the past/preterite verb 'they went/departed'. یکسر is an adverb ('entirely / one and all') modifying the action; classical Persian allows the verb before its adverbial modifier. گروها گروه is a repetitive nominal sequence (two synonyms in apposition) emphasizing 'groups upon groups' or many bands.
segments.s054-g36: پراگنده is an adjective/participle 'scattered' modifying the preceding groups; در + nouns gives locations 'in / across the plain, the sea, and the mountain' (در is the preposition 'in'). The coordinating و simply links the place nouns in the usual order.
segments.s054-g37: یکی 'a certain / one' introduces an individual. پرخرد is a prefixed adjective meaning 'very wise' (پر- intensifies). مرد is the noun 'man/person' and پاکیزه مغز is a phrase 'pure/clean of mind' with پاکیزه qualifying مغز (مغز here used metaphorically as 'mind/brain').
segments.s054-g38: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. بودش is بود + enclitic -ش (literary/colloquial) and here works like 'he had' / 'there was to him' (past copula with possessive sense). زبان 'tongue' = 'speech' and پر + ز = 'full of' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = poetic preposition 'of/from'), so گفتار نغز = 'delightful/pithy speech' is the content of that fullness.
segments.s054-g39: بگفتند is the past narrative 'they said'. تا here introduces the content/purpose of what they said ('that/so that'); زی is an archaic optative/exclamatory form 'live!' or 'long live' (imperative/wish) placed before the name as in formulas of blessing. منوچهر شاه is the addressee/title 'Manuchehr the king'.
segments.s054-g40: شود is the present/future/optative form 'may he become / he becomes'. گرم here is figurative 'ardent / fervent' rather than literal 'warm'. و links two wishes; باشد is a present/subjunctive copula 'may he be' (classical optative use). زبان سپاه 'the tongue/voice of the army' means 'the army's spokesman' or 'its voice' — زبان used metaphorically as 'speech/voice'.
segments.s054-g137: همان is a demonstrative ('that' / 'the same') placed before the noun نیکنامی ('good name, renown'). The phrase به و راستی coordinates two qualities: به (here a classical/prepositional/nominal item linking sense of 'goodness' or 'in') + و (the conjunction 'and') + راستی ('uprightness, truthfulness'). Together they form the noun phrase 'that renown in goodness and uprightness.' Word order is demonstrative → noun → modifiers.
segments.s054-g138: که introduces a relative clause referring back to the preceding noun phrase ('which/that'). کرد is simple past, 3rd-person singular ('he did/made'). The vocative ای پسر ('O son') interrupts the clause; برکاستی is a 2nd-person past form (archaic classical past) meaning 'you stripped off/removed/revealed' and takes سود ('profit, advantage') as its object — so the clause reads 'which he did — O son, you removed the profit.'
segments.s054-g139: منوچهر is the proper name (subject). بنهاد is a past tense verb ('he placed/established') here used as 3rd-person singular past. The direct object is تاج کیان ('the crown of the Kians') with the noun تاج followed by a genitive/attributive کیان ('the royal house / the Kians').
segments.s054-g140: بزنار (with a border/hem) qualified by خونین ('blood-red, bloodstained') forms an adverbial/attaching phrase describing how he treated the crown. ببستش is past (3rd-person) + clitic object -ش ('he bound/fastened it'). میان ('middle') indicates the location of that band — 'around its middle.' The verb precedes the prepositional/locative phrase as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s054-g141: برآیین is a prepositional compound meaning 'according to the custom/manner of.' It takes شاهان ('kings') as its genitive complement. یکی precedes the noun دخمه to mean 'one' or 'a certain' (indefinite 'a single'), and کرد is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'he made/did' — so 'he made a single tomb according to the kings' custom.'
segments.s054-g142: The correlative construction چه ... و چه ... (= 'whether ... or ...') introduces material/alternative: چه از زر سرخ و چه از لاژورد = 'whether of red gold or of lapis-lazuli.' از is the preposition 'of/from' marking the material, زر سرخ is 'red gold' (زر modified by سرخ), and لاژورد is the gemstone lapis lazuli.
segments.s054-g143: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/laid.' The sequence زیر اندرش is a fused locative: زیر ('under') + اندر ('in/within') + ش (3rd-person possessive 'his/its'), giving 'under it/him.' تخت عاج is the object 'ivory throne' (تخت 'throne' modified by عاج 'ivory').
segments.s054-g144: بیاویختند is past plural 'they hung/suspended.' The prepositional phrase از بر عاج ('from/on the ivory') uses a classical doubleness (از بر) to indicate position/attachment, with عاج qualifying تاج ('the crown'), so the clause states that they hung the crown on/over the ivory. Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase and the noun تاج serves as the object.
segments.s054-g65: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction meaning "when." پیروزگر is the agent noun "the victor/conqueror" in front of the phrase that follows. دادمان is a possessed noun built from داد ("justice") + -مان ("our") and combines with دستگاه ("system/establishment") to mean "our system of justice." The clause is compressed/elliptical (no finite verb until the next segment) — the temporal clause sets up the event described afterward.
segments.s054-g66: گنه‌کار پیدا شد is the main predication: پیدا شد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning "was found / was discovered" (پیدا = "found/visible", شد = auxiliary/past). گنه‌کار is the logical subject/thing found: "a guilty person/culprit." از + بی‌گناه marks the source/field — "from/among the innocent." The overall sense is that a culprit turned up among people who had been innocent.
segments.s054-g67: کنون روز opens with a temporal adverb + noun "now/day/era." دادست is a fused predicative form (داد + است) meaning roughly "it is (a) justice/day of justice," and بیداد شد follows with شد to mean "became injustice/oppression." The structure is contrastive: what was (day of justice) has, by becoming, turned into بیداد (injustice/tyranny).
segments.s054-g68: سران را sets up the affected people (سران "the leaders") with the object/topic marker را. سر here refers to their "heads" (literal or metonymic). از کشتن آزاد شد is a passive/affected construction: آزاد شد ("were freed/let go") with از کشتن indicating from what they were freed — "from being killed" — so the line says the leaders' heads were spared/exempted from killing.
segments.s054-g69: همه introduces the addressees "all/everyone." مهر جویید is an imperative/optative exhortation: جویید (present-subjunctive/imperative plural) "seek" and مهر "love/affection." و links to a parallel verb phrase: افسون کنید is another imperative plural (افسون "charm/enchantment" + کنید "do/perform") — "practice or use enchantment/charm." The two imperatives are coordinated instructions.
segments.s054-g70: ز تن is a prepositional phrase meaning "from the body" (ز = poetic "from," تن = "body/self"). آلت جنگ is a genitive/compound "instrument(s) of war" (i.e. weapons). بیرون کنید is the imperative plural "take out/withdraw." Put together the clause literally orders: "Take the instruments of war out of the body," i.e. lay down arms or rid yourselves of warlike implements.
segments.s054-g71: خروشی is a noun meaning "a shout/clamour/commotion." بر آمد (compound verb: بر + آمد) means "rose/appeared." ز پرده سرای is a source phrase "from the curtain of the chamber/room" (ز = "from," پرده = "curtain/veil," سرای = chamber/place). So the clause describes a noise or outcry coming from behind the curtain of the room.
segments.s054-g72: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory particle here (addressing persons) — an archaic poetic call "O!" پهلوانان is the vocative plural "champions/heroes." فرخنده رای is an adjectival phrase: فرخنده "auspicious/fortunate" qualifying رای "opinion/plan/counsel," so the addressee is praised as "fortunate in counsel" or "blessed in their counsel." The whole is a shouted address: "O champions, fortunate in counsel!"
segments.s054-g81: بفرمود is an honorific past tense verb meaning “he commanded/said.” It introduces a reported order. کان خواسته functions as the thing being commanded (literally “that desired/asked-for thing”), and برگرای is an older imperative form (second-person) meaning “return/bring back” or “turn back” that object; the imperative follows the nominal object in this poetic order.
segments.s054-g82: نگه کن is a compound-like imperative: نگه (root related to نگه داشتن ‘to keep/watch’) + the imperative کن ‘do’ → “keep/watch.” همه هر چه یابی is an object clause meaning “everything/whatever you find” (هر چه = “whatever/whatever thing”, یابی is present/subjunctive or simple present ‘you find’). به جای is a prepositional phrase “in/at its place.” Word order is typical Persian: imperative + object phrase + locative.
segments.s054-g83: به + پیلان گردونکش is a prepositional phrase qualifying the following noun: به (here ‘on/by/with’) + پیلان (elephants) + گردونکش (epithet ‘sky‑lifting’ or ‘heaven‑uprooting’, an honorific/poetic compound modifying پیلان). آن خواسته is the nominal head ‘that requested/desired thing’ which the preceding phrase attaches to (i.e. ‘that thing on/with the sky‑lifting elephants’).
segments.s054-g84: به درگاه = ‘to/at the presence (of the king)’ (به + درگاه). شاه‌آور آراسته is a descriptive phrase: شاه‌آور (regal/kingly) modifies آراسته (past participle ‘adorned’). Together they form a locative/predicative phrase: ‘adorned in/at the royal court/presence.’
segments.s054-g85: بفرمود تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning ‘he ordered that…’ (تا here functions like English ‘that/so that’ introducing the commanded action). کوس رویین و نای are the instruments/objects: کوس (war‑drum) qualified by رویین (‘iron/bronze’ → ‘brazen drum’), و connects to نای (‘ney/flute’). The phrase names what they were to sound.
segments.s054-g86: زدند is past‑plural ‘they struck/sounded.’ The conjunction و links the next clause; فرو is an adverb ‘down/away,’ and هشت پرده is the noun phrase ‘eight curtains.’ سرای here appears as a verbal/nominative element related to the hall/chanting (in the poem it functions idiomatically with پرده, i.e. the curtains of the hall or an action affecting them). Overall: ‘they struck/sounded, and (down) the eight curtains of the hall (were let down / were affected).’
segments.s054-g87: سپه را is the definite object ‘the army’ (را marks it). ز دریا به هامون shows movement from…to: ز (from) دریا (the sea) به (to) هامون (the plain). کشید is a past tense verb ‘drew/moved’ governing that motion; the pattern ز … به … + کشید indicates ‘moved/drew X from A to B.’
segments.s054-g88: This repeats the movement phrase but with new source and goal: ز هامون (‘from the plain’) سوی (‘toward’) آفریدون (name ‘Afrīdūn’). Again کشید is the past verb ‘he drew/moved (it).’ The preposition سوی marks direction toward the person/place named.
segments.s054-g105: پس is a discourse connective 'then/so'. پشت here is a locative noun 'the rear/behind' and is followed by شاه (king) as the reference point: 'behind the king'. اندر + ایرانیان places that whole locative phrase with respect to the group: اندر = 'among/amongst', so the full chunk means 'then, behind the king among the Iranians.' Word order is slightly compressed for poetry (locative + group).
segments.s054-g106: دلیران is the plural noun 'the brave ones' (subject/partners in the scene). و simply links clauses/phrases. هر یک = 'each one' (emphatic distributive). چو is the comparative/simile marker 'like/as'. شیر is the simile's noun 'lion'. ژیان is an archaic/poetic modifier attached to شیر; its precise nuance is literary (something like 'living/vigorous' or an intensifier), so it functions as a descriptive tag on 'lion' rather than a separate syntactic element.
segments.s054-g107: به پیش سپاه = 'at the front of the army' (به پیش = 'to/toward the front', سپاه = 'army/host'). اندرون here qualifies location 'within/inside' (often indicating the interior or the main body). پیل و شیر lists two kinds of animals/elements present 'elephants and lions' placed as those at the fore. The sequence is poetic but literal: 'in front of the host, inside, elephants and lions.'
segments.s054-g108: پس is the connective 'then/afterwards'. ژنده here appears as a descriptive modifier before پیلان; in classical dialects it would be a descriptive adjective but the supplied sense list doesn't offer a clear match (this is a poetic/archaic usage). پیلان is plural 'elephants'. یلان (plural of یل) means 'heroes' or 'mighty men'. دلیر is an adjective 'brave' modifying یلان. Together the phrase reads 'then (came) [the] ژنده elephants, the brave heroes' with poetic compression of syntax.
segments.s054-g109: درفش درفشان is a reduplicative poetic phrase: درفش 'banner' + درفشان as either an agentive or adjectival form ('shining/flag-waving' or 'the one that makes the banner shine'); this whole noun phrase names the banner/standard. چو آمد پدید = 'when it came to view/appeared' — چو = 'when', آمد = past 'came', پدید = 'to light/appear' (word order is inverted for effect: 'when appeared into view').
segments.s054-g110: سپاه منوچهر is the subject 'Manuchehr's army' (سپاه + proper name in apposition/possessive relation). صف بر کشید is a verbal phrase: صف برکشیدن = 'to draw up a line/compose the battle line' (صف = line/formation, بر is the particle that combines with کشیدن here to form the verbal expression 'to draw up/on', and کشید is the past tense 'drew/formed'). The verb is in the past, describing the army's action upon the banner's appearance.
segments.s054-g111: پیاده شد = 'he dismounted' or literally 'became on-foot' (پیاده 'on foot'; شد acting as auxiliary/past marker here). از باره = 'from the baggage/litter/vehicle' (از = from; باره = load/litter). سالار نو = 'the new commander' (سالار = commander/chief, نو = new). The whole clause states that the newly appointed leader got down from the baggage/litter.
segments.s054-g112: درخت نوآیین: درخت (literally 'tree') here names the pole or staff (standard-pole) and نوآیین is an adjectival compound 'newly fashioned/novel in ceremony'. پر از = 'full of' (پر = full, followed by the preposition از = 'of'). بار نو = 'new load/ornaments' (بار = burden/load/ornament; نو = new). The phrase describes the new ceremonial pole laden with new ornaments/loads.
segments.s054-g129: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. این کرده شد is a passive/perfect-like clause: این 'this/the foregoing' + کرده (past participle 'done') + شد used as auxiliary/past, so 'when this was done'. روز is the noun 'day' and برگشت بخت is a genitive compound 'the reversal/return of fortune' with برگشت (noun) modifying بخت.
segments.s054-g130: بپژمرد is an older/poetic past-form meaning 'withered' (the b- prefix here appears in classical narrative pasts). The following noun phrase برگ کیانی درخت is a poetic genitive sequence meaning 'the leaf(s) of the Kayanian tree' (noun–adjective–head order instead of modern ezāfe).
segments.s054-g131: کرانه 'edge/margin' is used metaphorically; گزید is past 'chose/selected' (here 'took himself to / withdrew to the margin/from the throne'). از بر is an archaic prepositional phrase 'from/on' with بر taking the meaning 'upon', so از بر تاج = 'away from the crown'. و گاه: و 'and' + گاه used adverbially 'occasionally/at times'.
segments.s054-g132: نهاده is the past participle/verb 'placed/put' (here 'placed on'). بر خود سر is a reflexive prepositional phrase 'upon their own head(s)' with خود marking reflexivity/possession and سر 'head'. هر سه شاه literally 'each/ every of the three kings' (هر + numeral + noun gives 'each of the three kings').
segments.s054-g133: پر here is the adjective 'full (of)'. The construction پر از X و پر ز Y uses two synonymous prepositions (از and poetic ز) in parallel: 'full of (blood of the) heart and full of tears'. خون دل is a compound 'blood of the heart' (figurative), و links the two parallel 'full' phrases; دو روی = 'two faces' (= both faces). Word order is poetic: adjectives before the noun they qualify.
segments.s054-g134: چنین 'thus/so' is a linking adverb. تا is the temporal conjunction 'until'. زمانه سرآمد is a noun + predicate: زمانه 'time/era' + سرآمد 'came to an end/conclusion' (here meaning 'when the time ended'). بروی is a narrative past/archaic perfective of 'go/leave' used here 'they departed/went away'.
segments.s054-g135: فریدون شد is a simple narrative clause 'Fereydun became' (implying he attained kingship). و links clauses. نام ازو ماند باز: نام 'name/reputation' + ازو (archaic 'of him') + ماند 'remained' + باز 'again' — together 'his name endured again' (i.e., his renown persisted).
segments.s054-g136: برآمد is the verb 'arose/appeared'. The following phrase برین روزگار دراز is a poetic nominal subject: برین is an archaic/dialectal form meaning 'this' (بر + این), روزگار 'age/time', دراز 'long' — so 'this long age arose' (i.e., the long era reasserted itself). Note the verb preceding its subject is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s054-g89: چو is the classical temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is simple past 3rd‑singular 'came'. به نزدیک تمیشه is a prepositional phrase 'near Tamisheh' (زبان کهن uses به + location), and باز (here) is the adverb 'again' placed after the locative phrase in poetic word order. Together they form 'when he came again near Tamisheh.'
segments.s054-g90: نیا را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker: 'Niyā (as object)'. بدیدار is the infinitival/nominalized form meaning 'to see/meet' (here functioning like 'saw' or 'laid eyes on'). او is the pronoun 'he/she' referring back to Niyā, and بد (adverbial) + نیاز 'need' form a predicate 'in bad need / in dire need'. The clause therefore reads 'he saw Niyā; he (i.e. Niyā) was in dire need.'
segments.s054-g91: برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth'. ز در is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the gate' (ز = از). ناله کر نای is a noun phrase 'the mournful/striking wail of the ney'; کر is an adjectival/poetic intensifier literally 'deaf' but here functioning to mean 'deafening/striking' or 'piercing' applied to ناله of the نای. Word order places the descriptive phrase after the verb, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s054-g92: سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely' modifying the verb بجنبید, which is past/plural or jussive in meaning 'stirred/moved/was roused'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host', and ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from (its) place'. The phrase thus states that the whole army was roused and moved from its place.
segments.s054-g93: همه پشت پیلان is a genitive phrase 'the backs of all the elephants' (همه = 'all', پشت = 'backs', پیلان = plural of 'pīl', 'elephants'). ز پیروزه تخت uses ز to mark material/ornament ('of' or 'made/laid with'): 'a throne of/laid with turquoise'. In classical Persian z can link material or specification to the following noun, so the segment describes 'a turquoise throne on the elephants' backs.'
segments.s054-g94: بیاراست is a past lexical form 'adorned / caused to be adorned' (here functioning as the main verb). سالار پیروز بخت is the subject/application of the verb: سالار 'the commander/chief', پیروز 'victorious', بخت 'fortune' — an epithet 'the victorious commander of Fortune'. Together the clause says that the victorious commander had the throne adorned/placed (on the elephants). Note classical Persian often places verb before its full subject phrase.
segments.s054-g95: چه is an exclamatory or emphatic particle here ('what/indeed' introducing description). با introduces an instrument/accompaniment 'with'. مهد زرین is a noun + adjective 'a golden cradle' (مهد = cradle, زرین = golden). به دیبای چین uses به + material/ornament phrase 'in/with brocade of China' (دیبای = brocade/silk, چین = China), describing the textile covering; poetic word order places the material phrase after the cradle.
segments.s054-g96: بگوهر is a prefixed participial adjective 'set with gems' (ب‑گوهر = 'with gems'), and بیاراسته here functions as an adjectival/verb form meaning 'adorned' (context: 'set and adorned with gems'). همچنین is a connective adverb 'also/likewise' tying this ornamentation to what was just described. Together: 'and likewise set (it) with gems.'
segments.s054-g57: چنین داد پاسخ که is a reported-speech formula: چنین (an adverb, “thus”) modifies داد (the verb “gave”) and پاسخ is its object → “gave an answer thus.” The conjunction که introduces the content clause. In the clause من کام خویش the pronoun من is the subject, کام is the noun “desire/wish,” and خویش is the possessive/reflexive (خوی + ـش) meaning “one’s own” — here ‘my own desire.’ Word order is literary: verb + object (داد پاسخ) followed by که + content clause.
segments.s054-g58: به خاک افگنم is a prepositional phrase (به + خاک “into/to the earth”) plus the 1st-person verb form افگنم; here the verb is an old/poetic 1sg form (optative/intentive) meaning ‘I will cast/throw (down).’ Then برکشم نام خویش contains another 1sg verbal form برکشم ‘I will raise/lift’ (literary/present-subjunctive shape) and the object نامِ خویش “my name” (خوی + ـش as reflexive/possessive). The two clauses are coordinated without a conjunction: “I will cast my desire into the earth and I will raise my name.”
segments.s054-g59: هر آن چیز کان is a literary relative construction: هر آن چیز “every/anything” followed by کان, an archaic/relative particle meaning roughly ‘which/is that,’ introducing the predicate. نز here is the poetic preposition ‘from’ (نَز ≈ از), ره is the archaic spelling of راه “way/path,” and ایزدیست is the predicate (ایزدی + است) meaning “is divine / is of God.” The overall literal order is: determiner + noun + relative particle + prepositional phrase + predicate.
segments.s054-g60: از آهرمنی‌گر z دست بدیست: از introduces the following agent/source (آهرمنی‌گر, “Ahrimanic/demonic”); the poetic preposition ز also means ‘from’ and combines with دست (“hand/power”) to mean ‘in the hand/power of.’ بدیست is the predicate with the copula (بدی + است) meaning ‘it is evil / it is bad.’ Grammatically this clause says that if the source is Ahriman, then the thing lies in the (evil) hand and is evil.
segments.s054-g61: سراسر ز دیدار من دور باد is an optative/wish: سراسر ز دیدارِ من = “throughout/from my sight” (i.e. “entirely from my sight”), دور is the predicate adjective “far/remote,” and باد is the optative particle (“may it be”). Note the poetic word order: the prepositional/genitive phrase precedes the predicate and باد marks the speaker’s wish.
segments.s054-g62: بدی را تن دیو رنجور باد is another optative clause. بدی (evil) is fronted and marked by the object/topic marker را for emphasis; تنِ دیو = “the body of the demon” (genitive relation), رنجور is the predicate adjective “afflicted/sickly,” and باد again gives the wish ‘may it be.’ The structure yields ‘May the demon’s body (and evil) be afflicted.’
segments.s054-g63: شما‌گر همه کینه‌دار منید: شما‌گر is a literary address/conditional marker (shifting to ‘you, if…’), همه ‘all,’ کینه‌دار ‘hostile/hating’ modifies the clause, من is the object ‘me,’ and the ending ـید on the verb indicates 2nd-person plural (you are). The phrase means roughly “if you are all my enemies.”
segments.s054-g64: وگر دوستدارید و یار منید: وگر is a conditional ‘and if/ but if’; دوستدارید is 2pl present ‘you love / you are fond of’ (the verb form ends in ـید for ‘you’ plural); و links the next clause, یار منید is ‘you are my companions/friends’ with یار ‘companion/beloved’ and من + ـید giving the 1st-person possessor / 2nd-person predicate structure. The elliptical parallelism (two alternatives: if you are enemies, or if you love/are friends) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s054-g49: گرش here is an archaic/clitic form meaning 'his (if his...)' (گر + ش), introducing a conditional 'if his ...'; رای is the noun 'counsel/plan' and جنگ است is the copula clause 'is war' (است = present copula). و joins the clauses; خون ریختن is an infinitival noun phrase 'the shedding/spilling of blood' (خون = blood(‑shed); ریختن = infinitive 'to spill/shed'), functioning in parallel with 'جنگ' as the content of 'رای'.
segments.s054-g50: نداریم is the first‑person plural present negative 'we do not have/possess' (نداریم = ندار + ایم). نیروی is the noun 'strength/force' used with an infinitive that follows; آویختن is an infinitive complement meaning 'to do/undertake (lit. to hang/attach)', so the whole phrase means 'we do not have the strength to (do) X' — the infinitive describes the action lacking force for.
segments.s054-g51: سران ('heads' or 'leaders') is the direct object here meaning 'heads' (collective); یکسره 'completely/all together' modifies how they will be brought; پیش شاه 'in the king's presence/before the king' places the action; آوریم is first‑person plural present/subjunctive 'we bring / let us bring' (classical Persian uses this bare form for the intended action). Word order is object + adverb + location + verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s054-g52: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'to him / toward him' (بر = toward/onto + او = him). سر بیگناه is a noun phrase 'an innocent head' (سر = head, بیگناه = innocent). آوریم repeats the same verb form 'we will bring' and completes the clause; the second clause restates the intended offering but specifies the innocent head as the object.
segments.s054-g53: براند is a third‑person verb (from راندن) used here in the optative/imperative sense 'let him take/have' or 'he may carry out'. هر آن کام 'every that desire' is an emphatic way to say 'whichever desire'; کو is an archaic relative particle ('that/which') introducing the relative clause (classical usage instead of modern که), and را is the object marker. هواست ('is his whim/desire') ends the clause: هوا = desire, ست = is (copula), so the clause literally says 'let him have every desire which his whim is.'
segments.s054-g54: برین is a fused form (بر + ین, where ین is an orthographic/poetic form of 'این') meaning 'on/for this' or 'therefore/in this matter'. بیگنه here means 'strange/alien' (an older adjective); جان ما 'our life' is the subject/possessed noun phrase; پادشاست is a predicative compound 'is king/sovereign' (پادشا = king, ست = is), so 'in this strange life of ours the king is sovereign' — a resigned statement of subjection. Word order is prepositional/emphatic fronting followed by subject and copula.
segments.s054-g55: بگفت is a simple past/perfect 'he said' (classical narrative past). این سخن 'these words' is the direct object. مرد بسیار هوش is an appositive noun phrase 'a man very full of intelligence' (= 'a very wise man'); note the inversion (verb before subject/object) is common in epic poetry, so the literal order is 'said these words a very wise man' but the natural reading is 'a very wise man said these words.'
segments.s054-g56: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander' (title). خیره is an adjective/adverb 'dumbfounded/astonished/staring' describing his reaction. بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (i.e., toward that speaker). دادگوش here functions as a verbal/periphrastic expression meaning 'gave ear/listened' (literally 'gave ear/hearing') — together the clause says 'the commander, astonished, listened to him.'
